DocumentationNarrative.Body:
Bulk import follows the same identity discipline as single-row insert and CSV import: row identity is explicit, stable, and part of the workspace semantics.
When importing flat files, keep source identifiers as stable `Id` values or scalar `...Id` landing fields first. Relationship promotion should happen after the landed workspace has complete referential-integrity evidence.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Bulk landing identity

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Insert many instances from tsv/csv input.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Check model and instance integrity.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Delete one instance.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Deploy SQL Server scripts.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Export instances as CSV.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
`meta generate csharp` emits dependency-free consumer types in a namespace that matches the model name (for example `namespace EnterpriseBIPlatform`):
- `<ModelName>.cs` (static model facade with a built-in instantiated snapshot, e.g. `EnterpriseBIPlatform`) - `<Entity>.cs` (one file per entity, POCOs)
If you also run `meta generate csharp --tooling`, `meta` emits a typed loaded model plus `<ModelName>.Tooling.cs` in the same model-name namespace. This tooling path is for workspace-backed runtime use; it does not emit the static built-in facade used by the dependency-free consumer path.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Generated C# surfaces

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Emit dependency-free POCOs and optional workspace tooling classes from the current model and instance graph.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
`meta generate sql` emits `schema.sql` and `data.sql` as deterministic SQL consumables.
The generated schema represents entities as tables, implicit `Id` values as primary keys, scalar properties as nullable or non-nullable columns, and required relationships as foreign-key columns plus constraints. The generated data script inserts the workspace instance rows in deterministic order.
This gives the same model and instance graph a database-native surface for inspection, interchange, and validation at scale.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Generated SQL consumables

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Emit deterministic SQL DDL and data scripts from the workspace so the same model can be inspected and loaded through a database-native surface.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Generate SSDT artifacts.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Show inbound relationships for an entity.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Show graph statistics.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Show help.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
`meta import csv` is Id-first: the file must contain a column named `Id` (case-insensitive header match).
On re-import into an existing entity, matching Id updates the row, new Id inserts the row, and rows missing from the CSV are preserved. Entity containers are always `<EntityName>List`. There is no alternate id-column mapping and no best-effort reconciliation.
Scalar landing fields that point at other entities should stay as `...Id` until promoted with `meta model suggest` and `meta model refactor property-to-relationship`.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Id-first CSV landing

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Import CSV data into a workspace.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Import SQL metadata into a new workspace.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Initialize a metadata workspace.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
Row `Id` values are stable strings; they do not need to be numeric.
Use explicit stable `Id` values whenever the source system already has identity. `--auto-id` is only for creating brand-new rows when no external identity exists.
Refactors, diff, and merge preserve row identities. They rewrite fields and relationships; they do not remap existing rows.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Row identity

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Insert one instance.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Diff mapped instance data using an explicit alignment workspace.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
Workspaces evolve independently on different branches. `meta instance diff` and `meta instance merge` are deterministic synchronization primitives for instance data:
- `diff` produces a structured change artifact from Left -> Right. - `merge` applies that artifact to a target workspace with strict validation.
This is not a text diff. It is a semantic diff over entity rows, scalar values, and relationship usages.
Inputs:
- Left workspace - Right workspace
Output:
- a deterministic diff workspace artifact (default sibling path: `<RightWorkspace>.instance-diff`)
Mechanics:
- requires byte-identical `model.xml` for equal-model diff. - compares row identity by entity + `Id`. - compares scalar property values. - compares relationship usage values (`...Id` attributes). - compares row presence/absence.
Conceptual change classes captured by the artifact:
- added rows (present in Right, absent in Left) - removed rows (present in Left, absent in Right) - updated scalar values on shared rows - updated relationship references on shared rows
Determinism guarantees:
- canonical ordering/normalization is applied before writing the diff artifact. - entity/property traversal and row comparisons are stable. - same inputs produce the same logical diff artifact.
The artifact is a structured description of how to transform Left into Right.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: What instance diff does

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Compare two workspaces with the same model and write a structured change artifact over rows, scalar values, and relationship usages.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Apply an aligned instance diff artifact to a target workspace.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
`meta instance diff` and `meta instance merge` are not:
- text merge tools. - automatic conflict resolution. - model/schema merge. - record matching by business key. - best-effort partial reconciliation.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: What this is not

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
Merge is strict. It fails rather than writing invalid state.
- required relationship usage missing -> validation failure. - relationship target row missing -> validation failure. - deleting rows that remain referenced -> validation failure. - relationship updates are applied as part of the snapshot and validated with the whole workspace.
Operation ordering is snapshot-based: merge builds the target end state in memory, validates, then saves. It does not persist partial intermediate steps.
Rows are matched by identity (`Entity`, `Id`), not by value similarity.
- identity stability is assumed. - no fuzzy matching. - no business-key reconciliation. - if a target workspace has drifted so that an incoming `Id` would collide with different state, merge fails its precondition instead of remapping or guessing.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Referential integrity and row identity

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
Inputs:
- target workspace - diff artifact workspace (from `meta instance diff`)
Operation:
- verifies precondition: target must match the diff Left snapshot. - applies the diff Right snapshot to the target in memory. - normalizes and validates the resulting workspace. - persists once only if validation/postconditions pass.
Atomicity and safety:
- all changes are applied or none are persisted. - any failure restores pre-merge in-memory state and aborts. - save is a single workspace write operation after successful validation. - rows created by merge keep the `Id` values recorded in the diff artifact. - merge never allocates replacement ids or remaps existing ids.
Determinism:
- same target + same diff artifact -> same resulting workspace state.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: What instance merge does

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Apply a structured instance diff to a target workspace only when its precondition still matches, so workspace changes synchronize without text merges.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
Example flow with two independently evolved workspaces:
1. baseline/integration workspace is your Left reference. 2. feature workspace with intended changes is your Right reference. 3. generate diff from Left -> Right. 4. apply diff into integration target (which must still match Left snapshot).
`meta instance diff .\WorkspaceA .\WorkspaceB` `meta instance merge .\WorkspaceA .\WorkspaceB.instance-diff`
If merge precondition fails, regenerate diff against the current target baseline.
`model refactor property-to-relationship` changes instance structure (scalar values moved to required relationship usages). Diff/merge captures and replays those instance-level structural value changes when both sides share the same model contract.
For model-contract drift between workspaces, use the aligned diff/merge path (`instance diff-aligned` / `instance merge-aligned`) with explicit alignment mappings.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Typical diff/merge workflow

DocumentationNarrative.Body: List instance relationships.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Set an instance relationship.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Target entity, relationship role, or implied relationship field name, followed by target instance id.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Rename one instance id.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Update one instance.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: List entities in the model.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: List properties for an entity.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: List relationships for an entity.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Add an entity.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Add a property to an entity.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Add a relationship.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Drop an entity.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Drop a property.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Drop a relationship.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
`meta model refactor property-to-relationship` performs an atomic model+instance rewrite from a scalar property to a required relationship.
The source property is dropped by default after the relationship usage is created. Use `--preserve-property` only when the scalar field name will not collide with the implied relationship usage name.
The command preserves row identities and rewrites each affected instance from scalar `...Id` data to relationship usage data. If the workspace cannot be rewritten and validated as one complete end state, the mutation does not persist.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Atomic relationship promotion

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Convert a property into a relationship.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
`meta model refactor relationship-to-property` performs the reverse structural rewrite: a required relationship usage becomes a scalar Id property.
The command preserves row identities and rewrites fields, not records. It is useful when a relationship should be flattened for interchange or when a model contract needs to move a reference back to scalar data.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Atomic relationship demotion

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Convert a relationship into a property.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Rename an entity.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Rename the model.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Rename a property.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Rename a relationship role.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Change whether a property is required.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Inspect landed scalar Id fields and print only relationship promotions with complete referential-integrity evidence.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
This is the intended landing workflow: import flat CSVs with stable row Ids, run suggest, then apply an atomic model+instance refactor.
`meta model suggest` is structural and deterministic. It prints strong exact-name relationship promotions by default and prints weak suggestions separately when RI is still complete but the target is only a role-style suffix match or the same source property matches more than one eligible target.
Eligible means the promotion satisfies 100% referential integrity (RI) before any mutation:
- source lands first as a scalar `...Id` field (for example `WarehouseId`) - target lookup property is complete and unambiguous (no null/blank values, no duplicates) - source property is complete (no null/blank values) - every source value resolves to an existing target lookup value (full coverage, no unmatched values) - source and target scalar types are compatible under strict rules (no implicit casting) - source values are reused, so the source behaves like a reference and the direction is structurally evidenced as many-to-one
For Id-first landing, the sanctioned structural rule is `ProductId -> Product.Id`, `SupplierId -> Supplier.Id`, and so on. `meta model suggest` does not rely on `Code` fields for this workflow. If any rule fails, the item is not printed.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: CSV import -> suggest -> refactor

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Search instances with equals/contains filters.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Show workspace summary and model/instance sizes.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: View one entity definition.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: View one instance row.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Merge two full workspaces into a new workspace.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body: Build deterministic metadata workspaces that can be reviewed in XML, projected into SQL, and consumed through typed C# tooling without changing the represented model.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Summary

DocumentationNarrative.Body:
Meta is the foundation tool for creating, evolving, inspecting, and projecting typed metadata workspaces. A workspace declares a model and its instance graph once, then makes that same structure available as deterministic XML, relational SQL, and typed C# without changing its meaning.
The CLI owns the everyday lifecycle: initialize a workspace, inspect its model and rows, make intentional edits, import a source surface, and generate a consumable representation. Model refactors rewrite the corresponding instance structure atomically, while structured diff and merge preserve stable row identities so workspace evolution can participate in normal code review and branch workflows.
The result is a durable model boundary. A database, generator, service, or analytical tool can consume a natural representation for its environment while staying connected to the same declared entities, properties, relationships, and data.
DocumentationNarrative.Title: Working with meta
